patterns.grammar_focus: will be / has been / is being + past participle
distractor_patterns: active voice instead of passive
wrong auxiliary verb (have vs. be)
wrong tense (present vs. future passive)
active form used as adjective
patterns.generation_instruction: Generate sentences about office procedures, documents being processed, or policies being implemented. Use future passive (will be + p.p.), present perfect passive (has been + p.p.), or present continuous passive (is being + p.p.). Context: internal memo, policy notice, or project update.

patterns.grammar_focus: noun vs. adjective vs. adverb form of the same root word
distractor_patterns: noun form where adjective is needed
adverb form where adjective is needed
verb form where noun is needed
similar-sounding different word
patterns.generation_instruction: Generate sentences requiring the correct word form (noun/adjective/adverb from same root). Common roots: compete/competitive/competitively, impress/impressive/impressively, produce/product/productive. Context: performance review, quarterly report, market analysis.

patterns.grammar_focus: present perfect vs. past simple vs. present perfect continuous
distractor_patterns: simple past when present perfect is needed (no specific time)
present perfect when simple past is needed (specific time given)
present continuous instead of present perfect continuous
past perfect where present perfect is correct
patterns.generation_instruction: Generate sentences testing the distinction between present perfect and past simple. Use time markers: since, for, already, yet, recently, last week, in 2024. Context: project status update, team email, progress report.

patterns.grammar_focus: in / on / at for time and place; by / until for deadlines
distractor_patterns: in vs. on for dates
at vs. in for locations
by vs. until for deadlines
for vs. during for duration
patterns.generation_instruction: Generate sentences testing preposition usage for time and place. Common pairs: in June / on June 5th, at the office / in Tokyo, by Friday / until Friday. Context: meeting scheduling, event planning, office directions.

patterns.grammar_focus: although/despite, because/due to, unless/as long as, therefore/however
distractor_patterns: although vs. despite (clause vs. noun phrase)
because vs. because of (clause vs. noun phrase)
however vs. therefore (contrast vs. result)
unless vs. if (negative condition vs. positive)
patterns.generation_instruction: Generate sentences testing logical connectors. Focus on pairs that are commonly confused: although/despite/in spite of, because/due to/owing to, unless/if/ provided that. Context: business decisions, policy conditions, project outcomes.

distractor_patterns: subject pronoun where object pronoun is needed
possessive adjective vs. possessive pronoun
reflexive vs. object pronoun
who vs. whom (subject vs. object of clause)
patterns.generation_instruction: Generate sentences testing pronoun usage in business contexts. Focus on: who/whom/whose, it/its/itself, they/them/their/themselves. Context: team communication, client interaction, department coordination.

distractor_patterns: that in non-restrictive clause (comma requires who/which)
which for people (needs who/whom)
what as a relative pronoun (not valid in standard English)
who vs. whom (subject vs. object of relative clause)
patterns.generation_instruction: Generate sentences testing relative clause usage. Key patterns: non-restrictive (comma + who/which), restrictive (that/who/which), possessive (whose). Context: describing company personnel, products, services, or policies.

distractor_patterns: similar-sounding word with different meaning (affect/effect)
same prefix different root (comply/compile)
wrong collocation (make vs. do a decision)
word with wrong connotation in business context
patterns.generation_instruction: Generate sentences requiring advanced business vocabulary. Focus on verbs: implement, allocate, negotiate, comply, acquire, submit, authorize, consolidate. Context: corporate policy, merger, financial report, legal compliance.

patterns.grammar_focus: past perfect vs. past simple; by the time + past perfect
distractor_patterns: past simple when past perfect is needed (earlier action)
past perfect when past simple is correct (standalone past event)
present perfect instead of past perfect (past reference point)
wrong form of past perfect (had + present instead of had + p.p.)
patterns.generation_instruction: Generate sentences testing past perfect tense. Use patterns: by the time X happened, Y had already happened; X had never happened before Y; after X had finished, Y started. Context: company history, project retrospectives, industry changes.

patterns.grammar_focus: dependent prepositions: responsible for, in charge of, capable of, interested in
distractor_patterns: wrong preposition with adjective (responsible for vs. responsible to)
wrong preposition with verb (apply for vs. apply to)
missing preposition where required
extra preposition where not needed
patterns.generation_instruction: Generate sentences testing preposition collocations. Focus on: adjective + preposition (capable of, responsible for, familiar with, interested in, similar to), verb + preposition (apply for, refer to, depend on, belong to). Context: job descriptions, business correspondence, proposals.
